coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and thermophysical property characterization of aqueous graphene quantum dot dispersions for air-conditioning applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Motamedi, Mahdi
Zheng, Xiaoran
Koshy, Pramod
Taylor, Robert A.
coredata.dc:description: Air-conditioning systems are on track to demand most of the electricity consumed by buildings around the world. The authors propose that dispersing quantum dots into the chilled water loops of air-conditioners represents a path towards improving the efficiency of air-conditioners. As such, the thermophysical properties of carbon-based quantum dot ‘nanofluids’ (e.g., nanoparticles dispersed in liquids) are presented in this study for sub-ambient temperatures (5–15°C)—an under-explored temperature range which requires understanding for air-conditioning applications. This study also explores dispersion stability and materials compatibility—another under-explored area in the literature which is required for commercial uptake. In this study, carbon quantum dots were synthesized via the hydrothermal route and characterized with UV–Vis, FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy, and TEM. Next, the thermophysical properties of specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and viscosity of the nanofluids were experimentally measured between 5 and 15°C (not previously reported for aqueous quantum dots). The highest thermal conductivity enhancement was ∼11% (compared to DI water) for 0.3 wt.% at ∼11 °C. Finally, the stability of the fluid was monitored over time and after exposing the fluids to common materials used in air-conditioning systems (e.g., copper, brass, and stainless steel). Unchanged UV–Vis spectra and the lack of sedimentation indicate that the developed dispersions are indeed suitable for chilled water air-conditioning applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot
Thermal properties
Compatibility
Stability
Dispersion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was done by a joint support of RCS-Air Pty Ltd and the Australian Government's AusIndustry Entrepreneurs' Programme (Innovation Connections). The authors would like to acknowledge the support of Tony Power and Jim Rich for initiating and supporting this study. The authors also acknowledge the facilities and the scientific and technical assistance of Microscopy Australia at the Electron Microscope Unit (EMU) within the Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre (MWAC) at UNSW Sydney. The authors also acknowledge use of facilities in the Solid State & Elemental Analysis Unit at Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre at UNSW Sydney. MM would like to thank the technical staff at MATSC and MME including George Yang, Pujith Vijayaratnam, Yuyan Yu, and Omear Saeed. MM also thank Mehdi Eshraghi for his advice on Scopus searching.

This research was done by a joint support of RCS-Air Pty Ltd and the Australian Government's AusIndustry Entrepreneurs' Programme (Innovation Connections). The authors would like to acknowledge the support of Tony Power and Jim Rich for initiating and supporting this study. The authors also acknowledge the facilities and the scientific and technical assistance of Microscopy Australia at the Electron Microscope Unit (EMU) within the Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre (MWAC) at UNSW Sydney. The authors also acknowledge the use of facilities in the Solid State & Elemental Analysis Unit at Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre at UNSW Sydney. MM would like to thank the technical staff at MATSC and MME including George Yang, Pujith Vijayaratnam, Yuyan Yu, and Omear Saeed, and Amr Omar. MM also thank Mehdi Eshraghi for his advice on Scopus searching.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the number of studies with ≥100 citations in which the thermal conductivity of water-/oil-based nanofluids was measured categorized based on their temperature ranges according to a Scopus search using “nanofluid” & “thermal conductivity” keywords on 15.09.2023.

Equipment used to measure thermophysical properties of the samples: a) Sub-ambient thermal conductivity setup (a KD2 Pro + a Styrofoam box, filled with ice, water, and a sample rack); b) Temperature-controlled viscometer setup, and; c) A DSC 1 (Mettler Toledo), crucible sealing press, furnace chamber, a sealed 40 µl aluminium crucible (from right to left).

Graphene quantum dot characterization: a) Scheme of the reaction, b) UV–Vis results for blue, green, red, and yellow samples, c) FT-IR spectra “Yellow”, “Red”, and “Green” samples, d) Raman spectrum of “Green” synthesized sample using 633 nm laser, 1% power, 10 s exposure, and 3 accumulations, e) Selected TEM image of the “Blue” sample, f) Selected TEM image of the “Green” sample, g) Selected TEM image of the “Yellow” sample, and h) Selected TEM image of the “Red” sample.

Thermophysical properties results: a) Thermal conductivity results at 4 different concentrations (pure water data from [43]), b) Viscosity results at 4 different concentrations (pure water data from [44]), c) Specific measurement “Evaluation” output curve taken from STARe software, and d) Specific heat capacity results using “direct method”, corrected by average DI water data taken from [29].

a) The Photo of fresh samples, b) Photo of the samples after ∼8 months, c) Comparison of light transmission of the samples for up to 7 days, and d) Perikinetic constant expressing Brownian motion intensity.

The results of compatibility tests of metal pieces exposed to the nanofluid samples taken at different time intervals together with the initial and final color of the fluid: a) Copper, b) Brass, and c) Stainless steel (S.S.).

ICP results: a) Metal pieces + DI water (in the absence of the particles) and b) Particle suspension (in DI water) + metal pieces (unexposed fluid was analyzed as the reference). Note: The typical uncertainty for ICP concentration values is ±2 % and the trendlines are included for visual guidance only.
Summary of selected studies on carbon quantum dots.

list-item.para: Amine-treated graphene quantum dots stable for 30 days
5.2% improvement in thermal conductivity at 100 ppm
Thickness < 1 nm & diameter of 5–20 nm (0.001 & 0.002 wt.%)
∼17% boost in thermal conductivity of 0.002 wt.% at 50 °C
Improved overall and tube-side heat transfer coefficients, effectiveness, and heat transfer rate (up to 1 vol% nanoparticles)
Highest coefficient of performance = 2.76, using 500 ppm amine-treated carbon quantum dots in polyalkylene glycol
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots further functionalized with NiCoP showed enhanced diffusivity and adsorption of methanol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between of the measured and modelled specific heat capacities of samples at ∼31 °C.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and thermophysical property characterization of aqueous graphene quantum dot dispersions for air-conditioning applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Manufacturing Engineering, UNSW Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Manufacturing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Mechanical and Manufacturing Engineering, UNSW Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, UNSW Sydney, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, UNSW Sydney, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Air-conditioning systems are on track to demand most of the electricity consumed by buildings around the world. The authors propose that dispersing quantum dots into the chilled water loops of air-conditioners represents a path towards improving the efficiency of air-conditioners. As such, the thermophysical properties of carbon-based quantum dot ‘nanofluids’ (e.g., nanoparticles dispersed in liquids) are presented in this study for sub-ambient temperatures (5–15°C)—an under-explored temperature range which requires understanding for air-conditioning applications. This study also explores dispersion stability and materials compatibility—another under-explored area in the literature which is required for commercial uptake. In this study, carbon quantum dots were synthesized via the hydrothermal route and characterized with UV–Vis, FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy, and TEM. Next, the thermophysical properties of specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and viscosity of the nanofluids were experimentally measured between 5 and 15°C (not previously reported for aqueous quantum dots). The highest thermal conductivity enhancement was ∼11% (compared to DI water) for 0.3 wt.% at ∼11 °C. Finally, the stability of the fluid was monitored over time and after exposing the fluids to common materials used in air-conditioning systems (e.g., copper, brass, and stainless steel). Unchanged UV–Vis spectra and the lack of sedimentation indicate that the developed dispersions are indeed suitable for chilled water air-conditioning applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the International Energy Agency's (IEA) “Future of Cooling” report, there are over 1.6 billion air conditioning units in operation, consuming ∼20% of the total electricity used in buildings globally [1]. Shockingly, this IEA report suggests that “left unchecked, energy demand from air conditioners will more than triple by 2050″ [1]. Without significant intervention, this trend towards ∼60% of building electricity being devoted to cooling could become a major barrier to achieving climate action plans and carbon-neutral building targets. Fortunately, there is still plenty of room for efficiency improvements in air conditioners since the average efficiency of the installed base is much lower (often less than half) of the ‘best available’ system in most markets around the world [1]. Thinking further ahead, the thermodynamic limit for the coefficient of performance (COP) of air conditioners is usually ∼4X higher than the ‘best available’ system (i.e., COPlimit ∼12 versus a modern system which has a COP of 3). Efficiency improvements can be achieved through modifying the design, optimizing the operation, or through modifying the underlying thermophysical properties of the materials used in these systems (i.e., the aim of this work). The authors propose that one low-hanging fruit option to boost system efficiency is to swap out the water in chilled water loops with water containing dispersed nanoparticles (often called nanofluids).

Nanofluids were initially introduced by Choi and Eastman in 1995 [2] and are defined as dispersions of nanoparticles, typically less than 100 nm in size. In the last ∼30 years, the subsequent research on nanofluids has been extensive [3]. Despite several early studies indicating large (anomalous) increases in thermal conductivity, a consensus seems to be arising that introducing nanoparticles can provide a modest heat transfer boost, but it may be counter-acted by viscosity increases and, importantly, fluid instability [4,5]. Therefore, a balance must be maintained between the improvement of thermal conductivity and increased viscosity and the potential loss of fluid stability [6].

At a fundamental level, nanoparticles in a dispersion experience competing fluid-induced forces (due to bulk flow and due to local Brownian motion), buoyancy, and gravitational forces. Taken together, the net result of these forces over time often leads to sedimentation of particles. However, Brownian motion can become dominant for small particles which do not frequently collide, leading to a stable dispersion [4,7]. Quantum dots, which are typically smaller than 10 nm (e.g., the carbon quantum dots reported by Xu et al. in 2004 [8,9]), represent a potentially stable choice for nanofluids. Along this line of research, Table 1 summarizes some of the recently reported water-based carbon quantum dot nanofluids.

Numerous reports are available in the literature on using nanoparticles in refrigerants, lubricants, and other heat transfer applications [17]. However, few studies have focused on using nanofluids in chilled water loops of air-conditioning systems. As evidence of this, a Scopus search was carried out using “nanofluid” and “thermal conductivity” to analyze the temperature at which thermal conductivity was measured for water- and oil-based nanofluids. The result of this investigation is illustrated in Fig. 1 , which indicates only a small percentage of studies provide property data below 20 °C.

In one recent, notable example, Ahmed and Elsaid reported a boost in the coefficient of performance of up to 24.3 % of a vapor compression system by using a hybrid nanofluid (alumina and titania nanoparticles dispersed in water) in the chilled water loop [18]. However, Ahmed and Elsaid also reported that shelf stability (at ambient temperature) was limited to 16 days [18].

For a typical chilled water loop, the operation temperature stays in the range of 4–15°C [19]. Due to lower frequency of particle-particle interactions, it can be expected that nanofluids will maintain their dispersion longer for this sub-ambient temperature range than in applications which operate at elevated temperatures [20]. For example, Wen and Ding reported instability of a carbon nanotube nanofluid containing sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) at temperatures 60–70 °C [21].

Another under-investigated issue is the materials compatibility between the nanoparticles and pipes, pumps, and fitting materials. Common materials that are utilized in HVAC systems are copper and steel [22]. Another prevalent material in any water system is brass [23,24].

Based upon a careful review of the pertinent literature, research gaps exist for the use of carbon quantum dots (often an order of magnitude smaller than metal oxides particles) in chilled water loops, along with key thermophysical properties (including viscosity and thermal conductivity) at the actual operating temperature of the application. To address this, the current study synthesizes and characterizes graphene quantum dot-water dispersions at sub-ambient temperatures. Another gap exists in studying the compatibility of the quantum dot nanofluids with the metals (copper, brass, and steel) used in chilled water loops. To address this gap, a full range of materials compatibility tests were conducted with both pristine and soiled/used metals.
Urea (U4884) and citric acid (C1909) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The chemicals were used as received.

To produce the quantum dots, 1.48 gr urea and 1.64 gr citric acid were dissolved in 40 ml of deionized water using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for 20 min. Then, the solution was poured into a 100 ml hydrothermal vessel. The vessel was heated in an oven in air at 160 °C (heating rate of 3 °C/min) for 2 h [25]. According to [25], it is expected that this method will produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) ranging from 2 to 5 nm in diameter. After the hydrothermal process, the product was either diluted (for direct use) or freeze-dried (for later use, using a Labconco machine at 0 °C for 12 h). In Fig. 3c, the samples named ‘Yellow- GQDs’ and ‘Red- GQDs’ were further modified by heating in an oven and a vacuum oven, respectively, at 80°C for 12 h. UV–Vis (Perkin Elmer Lambda 365), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR, Spotlight 400 FTIR, PerkinElmer Waltham, MA, USA, within the wavelength of 400−4000 cm−1), Raman analysis (inVia Reflex Raman 633 nm, 1% power, 10 s exposure, 3 accumulations), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200 Freising Germany) were used for further characterization of the samples.

To characterize the quantum dots, the dispersions were investigated with FT-IR, UV–Vis, and Raman spectroscopy. A freeze-dried sample was also analyzed using Raman. Finally, the average particle sizes were determined using TEM.

Various concentration of samples was prepared by re-dispersing the freeze-dried particles in an ice-bath with a high-power tip sonicator (UP200S from Hielscher). A KD2 Pro Thermal Analyzer was used to measure the thermal conductivity of samples (see Fig. 2 a). With this instrument, a KS-1 probe was inserted to a 15 ml-centrifuge tube placed in custom-built, insulated container filled with ice and water for a period of 48 h, with measurements every 15 min, to ensure data was recorded with minimal vibrations and minimal convective fluid motion. To ensure the probe tip did not touch the tube walls, the probe was taped from four directions. This device has ±5% error in the range of 0.2–2 W/m K [26].

The prepared sample was first utilized for the thermal conductivity measurements, followed by the viscosity measurements. To obtain sub-ambient measurements, a vibrating paddle viscometer (SV-10, from the A & D Company) was connected to a refrigerated bath (a Brookfield TC-650), see Fig. 2b. The temperature was decreased to ∼4°C, and then the bath was turned off and the viscosity measurements were automatically collected as the fluid slowly warmed back to room temperature, using the WinCT-Viscosity Ver.1.13 V software, with measurements every 10 s for each sample. One major source of error in this instrument is that the volume of liquid can impact the results. Although care was taken to ensure the correct volume was used in each test, the SV-10 manual notes that every mm in the liquid column above or below the filling line can cause up to 10% error [27].

The specific heat capacity of four samples (including DI water) was measured using a differential scanning calorimeter (a DSC 1 from METTLER TOLEDO, using the STARe software V.17.00) Fig. 2c. The direct method was selected with this instrument (which has an error of 3.7%, excluding the error of the balance) [28] and each sample was sealed in a 40 µl aluminum crucible and then heated from 25°-80°C at a heating rate of 10°C/min. The results were generated using 12.01 gr/mol as the molecular weight of the carbon nanoparticles and 18.01 gr/mol for DI water (at the particle loading fraction). The central generated result points were corrected using the average water data at the corresponding temperatures from the literature [29].

To monitor dispersion stability, three approaches were evaluated. First, the spectral transmission of the samples was measured for several concentrations. Then, they were measured after 1 week to compare with the initial spectra using a Perkin Elmer Lambda 365 (in a 10 mm cuvette with air as the reference). Second, the samples were analyzed via TEM to determine the particle sizes and the presence of any agglomerated clusters. Third, the ambient temperature shelf-stability of the samples was observed (photographically) over a period of 8 months.

For materials compatibility testing, the samples were exposed to pristine and non-pristine/used copper, brass, and stainless steel and photos were taken at different time intervals (0, 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000 s, and 24 h) to visually assess any deposition on the metal surfaces or agglomeration of the particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES)
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to visual tests, the exposed fluids were also tested with inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, with an Avio 550 Max Optical Emission Spectrometer from Parkin Elmer) to check for the presence of any metal ions (i.e., Cu from copper, Cu & Zn from brass, and Fe & Cr from stainless steel) after the exposure. It is hypothesized that these may interact with functional groups present on the particles, which could cause aggregation of the particles.

For these tests, two series of samples were compared, DI water exposed to the metals and 0.3 wt.% exposed to the metal pieces. The samples were taken from different time intervals of exposure to investigate the release of ions over time. To perform this analysis, 2 ml of samples were digested by ultra-pure nitric acid (0.3 ml), 0.05 ml of H2O2-(from Chem supply), and 0.2 ml HCl in a hot water bath for few hours. Then, just before the analysis, the digested acidic solution was diluted x5 with purified water.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) were synthesized using a hydrothermal reaction. After synthesis, the color was observed to change over time and in response to the thermal and atmospheric conditions. Fresh samples appear “Green” as shown in Fig. 2a. After freezing to 0°C under vacuum conditions, the sample appeared “Blue”. At 80°C, it turned “Yellow”. Finally, at 80°C under vacuum, it transitioned to “Red” which is shown in Fig. 2a.

The UV–Vis spectra analysis (and the visual color) provide a good indication of the size of the quantum dots, see Fig. 3 b [30]. Quantum dots are typically defined as having sizes below 10 nm, with corresponding absorption peaks below 700 nm [31]. The absorption peak of “Green” GQDs is located at 645 nm, suggesting the formation of quantum dots of small sizes [32]. Notably, this peak undergoes a blue shift to 601 nm when subjected to low temperatures and vacuum conditions, indicating that these factors promote size reduction. In contrast, as the temperature increases, the peaks exhibit a distinct red shift to 655 nm representing the increase in size [33], possibly due to the aggregation of quantum dots. Additionally, vacuum conditions can reduce particle surface energy to approach particle recrystallization [34], resulting in a peak shift to 660.

FTIR (Fourier-Transform Infrared) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the presence of functional groups within the synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Fig. 3c). In all samples, the distinct signals corresponding to the presence of C=C (observed at 1574 and 1000 nm) and CH (noted at 1460 nm) groups were clearly discernible, indicating the formation of graphene structures [35]. The “Green”-GQDs exhibited the emergence of signals associated with CH (at 2910 and 2850 nm) and CC (at 1360 nm) groups, along with intensified signals for C=C and CH groups. These observations likely signify an increased extent of graphene formation in “Green”-GQDs [36]. Furthermore, the appearance of new signals at 3049 nm for OH groups and 2351 nm for NH groups can be attributed to the presence of -COOH and -NH2 functionalities, respectively [37,38]. These findings provide evidence for the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms (resulting from aniline) and hydroxyl groups (due to carboxyl functionality) into the GQDs.

In the FTIR spectra of the “Red”-GQDs and “Yellow”-GQD samples, the absence of NH groups was noted, which can be attributed to the high temperature causing the removal of amine groups [39]. At these conditions, the “Yellow”-GQDs displayed an increased presence of CC bonds and -COOH bonds, suggesting the formation of chemical bonds among the quantum dots leading to their agglomeration.

The Raman spectra (Fig. 3d) provide additional confirmation of the presence of GQDs. The peaks observed at 1348 and 1585 cm−1 correspond to the D and G bands of graphene, respectively [40]. The D band is indicative of symmetry disruptions at defects and edges, while the G band arises from in-plane CC deformations [41]. The inconsistency observed between the D and G bands is linked to the formation of larger-size GQDs, as evidenced by the higher G bands [42].

The graphene quantum dot (GQDs) size was assessed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), with selected images shown in Fig. 3e-h. The “Green”-GQDs displayed a uniform size distribution with an average diameter of ∼3.5 nm. In contrast, the “Blue”-GQDs had an average size of ∼2.1 nm. The “Yellow”-GQDs exhibited a slightly larger size, reaching ∼4.2 nm, while concurrently forming a distinct morphology in the form of nanorods. This phenomenon can be attributed to the increased occurrence of CC bonding in FTIR, which leads to the attachment of dots to create rod-like structures. The “Red”-GQDs exhibited a slightly larger average size, ∼4.4 nm, with a consistent a more aggregated morphology. This observation suggests that a pre-treatment under vacuum conditions can effectively promote the uniform circular distribution of the quantum dots.

As noted in the literature review above, very few studies report thermophysical properties of nanofluids at sub-ambient conditions. This was confirmed in Fig. 1 above, which shows that the number of studies which measure thermal conductivity at sub-ambient temperature pales in comparison with other studies involving measurements at temperatures at or above ambient temperature. The insulated rig described above was used to measure thermal conductivity of the GQDs at various wt.% (0–0.5 wt.%) across the application temperature range (namely 4–12 °C). As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, a minor enhancement in the thermal conductivity from 6° to 12 °C was observed. This was most pronounced at 0.3 wt.%, which shows a relative improvement (versus pure water) of an average of ∼6.2%.

Similar to thermal conductivity, little data is available for sub-ambient viscosity, so Fig. 4b shows the experimental result for these samples from 4 to 14 °C. Viscosity is especially important in a flowing liquid because it affects the amount of required pumping energy. As mentioned above, the filling accuracy of the viscosity measurement is relatively low, so large error bars are displayed in Fig. 4b. Despite this, the trend lines are relatively clear and they indicate that all GQD wt.% samples had a lower kinematic viscosity than pure water data taken from [45]. Except for the highest concentration (0.5 wt.%), viscosity decreases with increasing wt.% of the GQDs. It is hypothesised that residual organics could have a lower viscosity than water at sub-ambient temperatures.

Specific heat was tested next. As can be seen in Fig. 4d, the sample with 0.5 wt.% GQDs shows a clear enhancement compared to the basefluid. Although graphene has a lower specific heat than water (i.e., ∼2 KJ/kg.K as compared to 4.2 KJ/kg.K for water), several studies in the literature have reported enhancement of specific heat capacity of nanofluid, which was often attributed to a “compressed phase” of the fluid at the solid particle surface [46]. However, the data showed that the specific heat capacity of 1.5 wt.% GQD was lower than that of the basefluid, which is in line with what can be expected [47]. The theoretical specific heat capacity of nanofluids can be estimated using mixing model equations, such as the following [47]:
(1)
(
ρ
c p
)
n a n o f l u i d
=
(
1 − φ
)
(
ρ
c p
)
b a s e f l u i d
+ φ
(
ρ
c p
)
n a n o p a r t i c l e
where φ represents the volume fraction of the particles in the fluid:
(2)
ρ
n a n o f l u i d
=
(
1 − φ
)
ρ
b a s e f l u i d
+ φ
ρ
n a n o p a r t i c l e

To compare the experimental results with the model, the first measured points of the samples were chosen. Then, graphite properties were assumed (density 2260 kg/m3 [48] & specific heat capacity 8.12 J/mol K [49]) at near ambient temperature (e.g., at ∼31.11 °C). The density of one GQD nanofluid sample was measured using an accurate 1 litre volumetric flask and a balance at low temperature (∼4–5 °C taken from a fridge). It was confirmed that the density of the sample is remarkably similar to that of water. Thus, water density was used as the dispersion density in the calculation. The results of this comparison are presented in Table 2 . It was found that a bias error of ∼0.122 existed in the specific heat capacity measured in the instrument, so all measurements were shifted down by this bias in the table.

Since the collision of particles can result in formation of larger clusters that can lead to instability of the dispersion, the stability was analyzed by determining the perikinetic collision constant. This can be estimated by a simple equation which has been assumed to be representative of the stability for a monodisperse suspension of 1 particle type [50]:
(3)
K =
8
k B
T
3 μ
Where K, kB, T, and µ are perikinetic collision constant, the Boltzmann constant (i.e., 1.380649 × 10−23 J/K), the absolute temperature (K), and the dynamic viscosity of the basefluid (Pa.s), respectively.

The results of the stability analysis are shown in Fig. 5 , which compares fresh samples to those which have been on the shelf for some time. In Fig. 5a and b, the images of fresh samples and 8 month old samples, indicate that no sedimentation was observed at the bottom of the translucent samples. However, the color change is evident in the samples, especially for samples with lower concentrations.

Fig. 5c shows the spectral transmission of samples with 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.75 wt.% GQDs. For this measurement, the root mean square error (RMSE) of the spectra for these samples after 7 days (except for the 0.75 wt.% sample, which was taken after 4 days) are approximately 0.001, 0.038, 0.007, and 0.004, respectively. This indicates very good stability since the transmission spectra lines were nearly coincident [51]. Fig. 5d plots the perikinetic collision constant with respect to temperature. It can be clearly observed that at higher temperatures, collisions would be much more frequent, as expected [52]. For chilled water applications, the temperature range of interest is confined within the red square of the figure. For this region in Fig. 5d, the perikinetic collision constant is one-half of the value at 30°C. Thus, it can be inferred that the stability of the nanofluid should be approximately twice as high at sub-ambient temperature as compared to applications which have an average operation temperature of 30 °C.

Now that the fundamental characterisation and property analysis has been conducted, an important applied question is whether these materials will be compatible with the metals commonly found in the application. Fig. 6 illustrates the images of compatibility tests that were undertaken in this study with copper, brass, and stainless steel (S.S.) pieces exposed to nanofluid samples separately, respectively. The images show no evidence of sedimentation on any of these metal pieces.
However, as can be seen in Fig. 6a- c, it seems that exposure to nanofluid to these metals resulted in faster color change. To investigate this color change, further chemical analysis is required.

Complementary to qualitative materials compatibility observations, ICP analysis was carried out to determine the concentration of Cu, Zn, Fe, and Cr ions presented in the nanofluids after exposure to the metals. The results of this analysis are shown in Fig. 7 .

Fig. 7a shows that the concentration of all expected metal ions increases after exposure to the metals in pure DI water. Fig. 7b shows how this compares for the GQD suspensions. Significantly higher concentrations of copper and zinc were observed (>100 mg/L). However, in some of the trends the initial metal ion concentration decreases after several hours (e.g., the grey Cr curve from exposure to stainless steel, S.S.), which could be due to the formation of some complexes with functional groups on the nanoparticle surface. The exact chemical reactions were outside the scope of this study, but the presence of these metal ions can explain the observed color change in the fluids after exposure to the metals. Since no particle sedimentation occurred, the GQDs were still deemed to be stable in the presence of the copper, brass, and stainless-steel pipes, pumps, valves, and fittings which can be expected to be used in the operating environment.

This study presents experimental results for the synthesis, characterization, and thermophysical properties measurement (at sub-ambient temperatures, a key research gap in the literature) of a graphene quantum dot water-based nanofluid. As a new set of data for a promising chilled water application, this study found that the hydrothermal route of synthesis can produce a sample with slightly enhanced thermophysical properties at the target temperature range. The TEM studies showed exceedingly small average particle size depending, although this changes as was also indicated by the color of the sample, spanning from approximately 2 to 5 nm. This study observed a slight relative improvement of ∼6.2% at 0.3 wt.% for the dispersion thermal conductivity. The specific heat capacity was measured to have a modest change, depending on the concentration of the sample. The kinematic viscosity of GQD samples turned was also less than the measured DI water, which might be attributed to the potential existence of excess organic still present in the final dispersion. Finally, a series of qualitative compatibility tests were done to check the propriety of the nanofluid for the real application. The ICP results showed that up to 1000 µg/L of copper and zinc ions was detected in nanofluids that were exposed to pieces of copper and brass, whereas (as might be expected) stainless steel did not interact much with the graphene quantum dot particles. In future studies, it is recommend to directly measure mass loss from the copper/brass samples to confirm the mass balance.

This study can be extended in the future by studying the effect of pH on the thermal performance & stability of the nanofluid together with further compatibility tests using non-pristine materials. Overall, this study highlighted the scarceness of experimental thermal conductivity data at below ambient temperature, and a lack of stability and materials compatibility studies was addressed within this work. As such, this study presents a stable nanofluid that can be used to enhance the thermophysical water properties in a chilled water loop, a key component of the ∼600 billion USD HVAC (in 2022 according to [53]).

Mahdi Motamedi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xiaoran Zheng: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Pramod Koshy: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Robert A. Taylor: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Robert A Taylor reports financial support was provided by the Australian Government's AusIndustry Entrepreneurs' Programme. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was done by a joint support of RCS-Air Pty Ltd and the Australian Government's AusIndustry Entrepreneurs' Programme (Innovation Connections). The authors would like to acknowledge the support of Tony Power and Jim Rich for initiating and supporting this study. The authors also acknowledge the facilities and the scientific and technical assistance of Microscopy Australia at the Electron Microscope Unit (EMU) within the Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre (MWAC) at UNSW Sydney. The authors also acknowledge the use of facilities in the Solid State & Elemental Analysis Unit at Mark Wainwright Analytical Centre at UNSW Sydney. MM would like to thank the technical staff at MATSC and MME including George Yang, Pujith Vijayaratnam, Yuyan Yu, and Omear Saeed, and Amr Omar. MM also thank Mehdi Eshraghi for his advice on Scopus searching.
